title: The tram driver who walks his route
summary: Once a year, a retired Lisbon tram driver walks the full length of his old route on foot, from one terminus to the other.
body:
Senhor Almeida drove the number 28 tram in Lisbon for thirty-one years. He retired in 2017. The 28 is the famous yellow tram that climbs from Martim Moniz through Graça and the Alfama district, drops down to the river, then continues to Prazeres on the western side. It is on every guidebook. For most of his career it was not on guidebooks. It was a tram for people who lived along it and needed to get home with shopping.

Each year, on the first Saturday of October, Senhor Almeida walks the route. Not the rails — he walks the streets the tram passes through, doorway by doorway, in the order the tram would meet them. The full walk takes him about eleven hours, including stops. He started this on the first October after he retired, partly as a way to mark the year, partly because his doctor had told him that walking would keep the swelling in his ankles down. He has walked it eight times now.

The route has changed in ways he can list. Two of the cafés where he used to stop for a quick coffee between turns have closed and become souvenir shops. The grocer at the foot of the steepest hill, where he often bought oranges, is now a clothing rental for tourist photographs. The hardware shop near Graça closed in 2020 and the small bookshop that took its place closed in 2022. The bakery on rua das Escolas Gerais is still open, although it now does most of its trade with people who do not live in the building above it.

Other things have stayed. The woman at the laundry on the corner of largo das Portas do Sol still leans out of her doorway to wave at him, although he has been retired for nearly a decade and most of her current customers are short-stay visitors. A boy he remembers as a small child, who used to ride the tram with his grandmother, now runs a bicycle repair shop two streets above the cathedral. The boy is forty-one. The grandmother died in 2019. They had a coffee together on the bench outside the shop, in silence, for about twenty minutes.

Senhor Almeida is not, he insists, walking the route to mourn it. The walk is more like an inventory. He notices what has gone, what has stayed, what has arrived. He has a small notebook in his back pocket, which he fills with one or two sentences at each stop he used to call. He has never shown the notebooks to anyone, although his daughter once read a page over his shoulder and asked why he wrote so little. He said the point was not to write everything. It was to write the one thing he would otherwise forget.

Next October he will walk it for the ninth time. He has told the bakery he is coming. He has not told anyone else.

annotations.meaningVi: tên một quảng trường ở Lisbon
đi qua từng cửa nhà một
tiệm cho thuê quần áo để chụp ảnh du lịch
tiệm đồ kim khí / vật liệu xây dựng

grammarNotes.title: Used to + V and relative clauses (who / where) for description
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng 'used to + V' để chỉ thói quen hoặc trạng thái quá khứ giờ không còn, và mệnh đề quan hệ (who / where / which) để mô tả người, nơi chốn. Trong bài: 'Two of the cafés where he used to stop for a quick coffee...' (where = mệnh đề quan hệ chỉ nơi; used to = thói quen cũ). 'The grocer at the foot of the steepest hill, where he often bought oranges, is now a clothing rental.' (where = mệnh đề bổ sung). 'A boy he remembers as a small child, who used to ride the tram with his grandmother, now runs a bicycle repair shop.' (who = mệnh đề chỉ người; used to = thói quen cũ; hiện tại 'now runs' tương phản). 'The bakery on rua das Escolas Gerais is still open, although it now does most of its trade with people who do not live in the building above it.' (who = mệnh đề chỉ người).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses 'used to + V' for past habits/states no longer true, and relative clauses (who / where / which) to describe people and places. In the text: 'Two of the cafés where he used to stop for a quick coffee...' (where = place clause; used to = old habit). 'The grocer at the foot of the steepest hill, where he often bought oranges, is now a clothing rental.' (additional info). 'A boy he remembers as a small child, who used to ride the tram with his grandmother, now runs a bicycle repair shop.' (who = people; used to vs. now contrast). 'The bakery... is still open, although it now does most of its trade with people who do not live in the building above it.' (who = people).

translationVi:
Ông Almeida lái tàu điện số 28 ở Lisbon trong ba mươi mốt năm. Ông nghỉ hưu năm 2017. Số 28 là chiếc tàu điện vàng nổi tiếng leo từ Martim Moniz qua Graça và khu phố cổ Alfama, đổ xuống bờ sông rồi tiếp tục đến Prazeres ở phía Tây. Nó có trong mọi sách hướng dẫn du lịch. Phần lớn sự nghiệp của ông, nó không có trong sách hướng dẫn. Nó là tàu của những người sống dọc tuyến và cần về nhà với túi đồ chợ.

Mỗi năm, vào thứ Bảy đầu tiên của tháng Mười, ông Almeida đi bộ tuyến đó. Không đi trên đường ray — ông đi các con phố tàu đi qua, từng cửa nhà một, theo thứ tự tàu sẽ gặp. Cả chuyến đi mất khoảng mười một giờ, kể cả nghỉ. Ông bắt đầu việc này từ tháng Mười đầu tiên sau khi nghỉ hưu, một phần để đánh dấu năm, một phần vì bác sĩ bảo đi bộ sẽ giúp giảm sưng mắt cá. Đến nay ông đã đi tám lần.

Tuyến đã đổi theo những cách ông liệt kê được. Hai quán cà phê nơi ông thường dừng uống nhanh giữa các chuyến quay đầu giờ đã đóng và thành cửa hàng quà lưu niệm. Tiệm tạp hoá dưới chân dốc cao nhất, nơi ông thường mua cam, giờ là tiệm cho thuê quần áo chụp ảnh du lịch. Tiệm đồ sắt gần Graça đóng năm 2020, và tiệm sách nhỏ thay thế nó đóng năm 2022. Tiệm bánh trên rua das Escolas Gerais vẫn mở, dù phần lớn doanh thu nay từ người không sống trong toà nhà trên nó.

Nhiều thứ khác vẫn còn. Bà ở tiệm giặt góc largo das Portas do Sol vẫn nghiêng người ra cửa vẫy chào ông, dù ông đã nghỉ gần một thập kỷ và phần lớn khách hiện tại là khách ở ngắn ngày. Một cậu bé ông nhớ là đứa nhỏ, từng đi tàu cùng bà ngoại, giờ điều hành một tiệm sửa xe đạp cách nhà thờ hai phố lên trên. Cậu giờ bốn mươi mốt. Bà ngoại mất năm 2019. Hai người uống cà phê trên ghế trước tiệm, im lặng, khoảng hai mươi phút.

Ông Almeida khẳng định ông không đi để than thở về tuyến. Cuộc đi giống một bản kiểm kê hơn. Ông để ý những gì đã mất, những gì còn, những gì mới đến. Trong túi sau ông có một cuốn sổ nhỏ, ông ghi một hai câu tại mỗi điểm ông từng dừng. Ông chưa từng đưa các cuốn sổ cho ai xem, dù con gái có lần đọc qua vai một trang và hỏi sao ông viết ít vậy. Ông nói mục đích không phải viết mọi thứ. Mà viết duy nhất một điều mà nếu không, ông sẽ quên.

Tháng Mười tới ông sẽ đi lần thứ chín. Ông đã báo cho tiệm bánh ông sẽ đến. Ông chưa nói với ai khác.

questions.prompt: How long did Senhor Almeida drive the 28 tram?

options: Yellow tram from Martim Moniz through Graça and Alfama, down to the river, to Prazeres on the western side

questions.prompt: How was the 28 different in his career?
options: It used to be a tram for people who lived along it and needed to get home with shopping — not in guidebooks

questions.prompt: When and how does he walk the route?
options: First Saturday of October each year, on foot along the streets the tram passes, doorway by doorway, about 11 hours including stops

questions.prompt: Why did he start the walks?
options: Partly to mark the year, partly because his doctor said walking would keep ankle swelling down

questions.prompt: How many walks has he done so far?
What changes does he list along the route?
options: Two cafés → souvenir shops; the grocer → tourist-clothing rental; hardware shop closed 2020; bookshop replacing it closed 2022; bakery still open but mostly serving non-residents

questions.prompt: Who still waves at him from the laundry?
options: The woman at the laundry on the corner of largo das Portas do Sol, although most of her customers are now short-stay visitors

questions.prompt: What happened with the boy and his grandmother?
options: The boy (now 41) runs a bicycle repair shop two streets above the cathedral; his grandmother died in 2019; he and Senhor Almeida had coffee on the bench in silence for about 20 minutes

questions.prompt: Why does he say he is walking, if not to mourn?
options: It is more like an inventory — he notices what has gone, what has stayed, what has arrived

questions.prompt: What is in the notebook, and why so little?
options: One or two sentences at each old stop; the point is not to write everything, only the one thing he would otherwise forget
A retired driver keeps a private yearly ritual along his old route to take stock of what has changed and what has not — small, deliberate noticing instead of nostalgia.
Trams should be replaced by buses.

cloze.template:
Senhor Almeida drove the number 28 tram in Lisbon for thirty-one years. He retired in 2017. The 28 is the famous yellow tram that climbs from Martim Moniz through Graça and the Alfama district, drops down to the river, then continues to {{b1}} on the western side. It is on every guidebook. For most of his career it was not on guidebooks. It was a tram for people who lived along it and needed to get home with shopping.

Each year, on the first Saturday of October, Senhor Almeida walks the route. Not the {{b2}} — he walks the streets the tram passes through, doorway by doorway, in the order the tram would meet them. The full walk takes him about eleven hours, including stops. He started this on the first October after he retired, partly as a way to mark the year, partly because his doctor had told him that walking would keep the {{b3}} in his ankles down. He has walked it {{b4}} times now.

The route has changed in ways he can list. Two of the cafés where he used to stop for a quick coffee between turns have closed and become {{b5}} shops. The grocer at the foot of the steepest hill, where he often bought oranges, is now a clothing rental for tourist photographs. The hardware shop near Graça closed in 2020 and the small bookshop that took its place closed in 2022. The {{b6}} on rua das Escolas Gerais is still open, although it now does most of its trade with people who do not live in the building above it.

Other things have stayed. The woman at the {{b7}} on the corner of largo das Portas do Sol still leans out of her doorway to wave at him, although he has been retired for nearly a decade and most of her current customers are short-stay visitors. A boy he remembers as a small child, who used to ride the tram with his grandmother, now runs a bicycle {{b8}} shop two streets above the cathedral. The boy is forty-one. The grandmother died in 2019. They had a coffee together on the bench outside the shop, in silence, for about twenty minutes.

Senhor Almeida is not, he insists, walking the route to mourn it. The walk is more like an {{b9}}. He notices what has gone, what has stayed, what has arrived. He has a small notebook in his back pocket, which he fills with one or two sentences at each stop he used to call. He has never shown the notebooks to anyone, although his daughter once read a page over his shoulder and asked why he wrote so little. He said the point was not to write everything. It was to write the one thing he would otherwise {{b10}}.

Next October he will walk it for the {{b11}} time. He has told the bakery he is coming. He has not told anyone else.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Senhor Almeida writes only the one thing he would otherwise forget at each old stop. If you walked the route of a part of your life that has changed — a school, a neighbourhood, an old workplace — what is the one sentence you would write at each stop, and why that one and not another?
